confidence_rationale: Venkatesh and Malikopoulos (2025) directly address the problem that is most proximate to SD-013: model-based RL under confounding. They prove that transition and reward models learned from confounded observational data are fundamentally inconsistent -- they do not converge to the interventional quantities needed for planning under state-based policies. This is the closest available computational paper to the SD-013 claim, which is specifically about a forward model (E2_harm_s) rather than a policy. The paper's central negative result -- that conventional model-learning from confounded data misrepresents the effect of candidate policies -- directly instantiates SD-013's claim about causal_sig. The proposed remedy (surrogate MDP with proximal adjustment) is technically different from ARC-033's counterfactual perturbation approach, but the necessity argument (observational model is insufficient) is the same. Confidence is moderate because the paper is an arXiv preprint (not peer-reviewed at top venue) and the specific model class (forward model for harm stream) differs from the paper's setting (reward/transition models for planning).
failure_signatures: Venkatesh and Malikopoulos show that a transition model learned from confounded offline data generates systematically biased predictions for any action that deviates from the behavioral policy. For E2_harm_s, this means that z_harm_s predictions for a_cf -- which by definition deviates from the agent's behavioral policy -- will be biased toward the behavioral policy's harm-correlated outcomes. The causal_sig = E2_harm_s(z_t, a_actual) - E2_harm_s(z_t, a_cf) will compress toward zero because both predictions are biased by the same confounding pattern.

The paper establishes that the learned model is inconsistent: it does not converge to the true interventional distribution P(s' | do(a)) even with infinite observational data. This is a qualitatively stronger failure than noise or approximation error -- the bias is structural and irreducible without adjustment. In the REE context, this means no amount of additional observational training will produce a reliable E2_harm_s without counterfactual perturbation data.

The paper notes that conventional maximum likelihood model-learning in confounded C-MDPs fails because the behavioral policy's correlation with the unobserved context corrupts the model's implicit assumptions about action-outcome independence. E2_harm_s training using standard next-step prediction loss on confounded trajectories makes exactly the assumptions the paper proves are invalid.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In contextual MDPs where the context is unobserved and induces confounding in the offline dataset, transition and reward models learned directly from confounded data are fundamentally inconsistent: they do not represent the interventional quantities P(s' | do(a)) required for evaluating state-based policies. Conventional model-learning methods generate biased surrogates because the behavioral policy's correlation with the unobserved context contaminates the model's learned action-outcome associations. A surrogate MDP with proximal adjustment recovers consistent causal estimates.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-013 maps directly: E2_harm_s is a transition model (it maps (z_t, a) to predicted z_harm_s trajectories) and it must be trained from data generated by a behavioral agent whose policy is correlated with harm-proximal state occupancy. Venkatesh and Malikopoulos' inconsistency result applies directly -- the learned E2_harm_s will not converge to P(z_harm_s | do(a)). The causal_sig = E2_harm_s(z_t, a_actual) - E2_harm_s(z_t, a_cf) will be unreliable wherever the behavioral policy's confounding is strong. ARC-033's counterfactual perturbation steps break the confounding by collecting data under a policy that is orthogonal to the behavioral correlations -- the interventional sample counterpart to the proximal adjustment proposed in the paper.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper's proposed solution (proximal adjustment using proxy variables) differs technically from ARC-033's approach (explicit counterfactual execution). Both are valid remedies for the same underlying identifiability problem, but they make different observability assumptions. ARC-033 assumes the agent can execute a_cf in the actual environment; the proximal approach assumes access to proxy variables that are correlated with the unobserved context. For E2_harm_s training, the ARC-033 approach is more direct. The paper is also an arXiv preprint from late 2025 and has not undergone peer review at a major venue.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical ML paper; arXiv preprint (December 2025). Contextual MDP framework, offline dataset, model-based RL with confounding. Authors at University of Delaware. No biological or clinical context.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality is moderate: rigorous theoretical work but arXiv preprint, not yet peer-reviewed at a top venue. Mapping fidelity is the highest of the three entries because the paper directly addresses forward model (transition model) training under confounding -- the closest structural match to E2_harm_s. Transfer risk is very low: the model mismatch (general reward+transition vs. harm-stream only) does not affect the core inconsistency argument.
